planting.note: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; prairie lily is native to the limestone prairie soils and rocky hillsides of central and south Texas, where it blooms reliably after summer rain events — the characteristic 'rain lily' flush of flowers appearing 5–7 days after heavy summer rainfall. It is one of the largest-flowered Zephyranthes species, with white to very pale pink, upright, funnel-shaped flowers 3–4 inches across. Heat- and drought-tolerant once established; naturalizes freely in Gulf Coast landscapes.

directions: Plant bulbs in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; prairie lily is the largest-flowered native rain lily of Texas and the central Gulf Coast, producing in summer — typically 5–7 days after heavy rainfall events — large, upright, pure-white to very pale blush-pink, funnel-shaped flowers up to 4 inches across on stems 10–16 inches tall, each flower facing upward and outward with six broad, satiny white tepals of clean elegance. The rain-triggered flowering mechanism is one of the most distinctive behaviors of the Zephyranthes and Cooperia genera: the plants remain dormant through dry periods and flower synchronously with dramatic precision following significant rainfall, creating spectacular mass-blooming events across the central Texas prairies and limestone hills. In garden plantings, a deep summer watering can trigger the same response. The grassy, bright-green leaves emerge before or with the flowers and persist through the growing season. Native from Texas to northern Mexico, it is extraordinarily heat-tolerant and drought-adapted, requiring virtually no supplemental irrigation once established in the Gulf Coast climate where summer rains naturally trigger its bloom cycle. An outstanding, low-maintenance native bulb for zone 9a Gulf Coast gardens.

planting.note: Plant small bulbs 2–3 inches deep in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; copper rain lily blooms in late summer following rain events, similarly to zephyranthes rain lilies, producing distinctive funnel-shaped flowers held at an angle (not fully upright) in warm coppery-orange to reddish-orange on the outer surfaces, yellow inside. It naturalizes freely by seed in the Gulf Coast region and can become a pleasant garden colonizer in lawns and dry borders.

directions: Plant small bulbs in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; copper rain lily is a South American Habranthus species that has naturalized across the Gulf Coast states from Texas to Florida and distinguished itself from the white zephyranthes rain lilies by its warm, coppery-orange to burnt-orange and yellow flower color — a unique warm-toned rain lily that fills a color niche (orange-copper funnel flowers) that no other commonly grown species covers. The flowers are held at a distinctive 45-degree angle (Habranthus, meaning 'graceful flower', characteristically tilts its flowers rather than holding them erect like Zephyranthes), giving each stem a graceful, nodding poise rather than the stiffly upright stance of Zephyranthes. The outer surfaces of the tepals are a warm reddish-copper to coppery-orange; the inner faces are yellow, creating a bicolor effect when viewed from below. Like its zephyranthes relatives, it flowers in flushes following summer rain events, producing masses of small, coppery blooms simultaneously across a naturalized planting. It self-seeds freely in Gulf Coast conditions and can colonize dry lawns and open garden areas without supplemental care. A delightful, self-sustaining small bulb for naturalistic and lawn-edge plantings.

planting.note: Plant bulbs with the neck at or just above the soil surface in moist to well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Orange River lily is one of the most cold-hardy crinums — surviving to zone 7b with winter mulching. It is native to the banks of the Orange River in South Africa and tolerates both periodic flooding and significant drought. One of the parent species of the hybrid Crinum x powellii. All parts are toxic if ingested.

directions: Plant bulbs (neck exposed) in moist to well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Orange River lily is the South African crinum that gives its extraordinary cold-hardiness to the widely grown hybrid Crinum x powellii (which has C. bulbispermum as one parent) — surviving to zone 7b, significantly colder than most tropical crinum species, and tolerating the brief hard frosts of zone 7 winters with root protection. The flowers are white to pale pink with a deep-rose central stripe on each outer petal surface — borne in summer (July–September) in umbels of 6–15 large, funnel-shaped, lily-like blooms on stout, hollow scapes up to 3 feet tall, sweetly fragrant in the evening. The broad, bright-green, strap-shaped leaves form an impressive, fountain-like rosette. It grows naturally along the Orange River in the Northern Cape, where it tolerates periods of seasonal flood and extreme drought, giving it exceptional adaptability in cultivation. Outstanding for zone 9a Gulf Coast gardens where large, fragrant, summer-blooming bulbs with tropical presence are needed — and the superior cold-hardiness compared to other crinums makes it a reliable garden perennial rather than a tender-treated container plant.

planting.note: Plant bulbs just below the soil surface in moist, deeply humus-rich, well-drained but moisture-retaining, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part to full shade; giant Himalayan lily is monocarpic — the main bulb flowers once then dies, but produces offsets that will flower in 3–5 years. Plant in groups of bulbs of varying sizes to ensure annual flowering. Very deep, organic soil is essential — it grows naturally in cool, moist, humus-rich forest floors of the Himalayas and needs consistent moisture and shade.

directions: Plant bulbs near the soil surface in deeply humus-rich, moist, well-drained but moisture-retentive, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part to full shade; giant Himalayan lily is possibly the most spectacular of all woodland bulbs — a monocarpic lily relative from the cool, moist, deep-shade forest floors of the Himalayas and western China that produces, after 4–7 years of vegetative growth, a towering flowering stem of almost incomprehensible scale: a stout, hollow scape up to 10–12 feet tall bearing in midsummer a succession of 15–20 large, white, outward-facing, funnel-shaped, strongly fragrant lily flowers, each marked with a deep wine-red to maroon streak down the inner throat — the whole structure an architectural titan of the summer shade garden, visible from considerable distance, unique in the garden world. After flowering and seeding, the main bulb dies, but it produces several offset bulblets that will grow for years before themselves flowering. A colony planted with offsets of varying sizes provides a succession of annual flowering stems. Despite its imposing stature, it demands the moist, cool, deeply organically-rich conditions of its Himalayan forest habitat — consistently moist soil, deep shade, and cool summers. Not suitable for hot, dry gardens. For appropriate cool, shaded, woodland gardens in zones 7–9, it represents one of the ultimate spectacles of the bulb world.

planting.note: Plant bulbs with the neck just above the soil surface in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Caribbean spider lily is native to the islands of the Caribbean and produces large, white, spider-like flowers with the characteristic Hymenocallis 'cup and strap' form — a central funnel-cup connecting the bases of long, narrow, spreading, strap-like perianth segments. It is strongly fragrant in the evening. In zone 9 it remains in the ground as a perennial; bring indoors in winter in colder zones.

directions: Plant bulbs in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Caribbean spider lily is a bold, tropical Hymenocallis species from the Caribbean islands — producing in summer umbels of large, pure-white, powerfully fragrant, spider-lily flowers in the distinctive Hymenocallis form: a central, flared funnel-cup at the heart of the flower, from the margins of which radiate six long, narrow, reflexed, strap-like perianth segments creating the signature spider-lily silhouette of a central cup surrounded by long, radiating white streamers. Each flower in the 3–8 flower umbel spans 6–8 inches across; a mature clump in bloom is a striking tropical display. The fragrance is very strong and sweet, most intense in the evening, carrying through the summer garden air. The broad, bright-green, strap-shaped, glossy leaves form handsome clumps even when not in flower — providing bold, tropical-looking foliage throughout the summer. It thrives in the warm, humid summer conditions of zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens in partial shade, where it naturalizes into ever-larger, more floriferous clumps over time. Outstanding combined with other bold, tropical-looking shade plants — cannas, elephant ears, gingers — for a lush, tropical summer border.
